candidate_problems.text: How far can Gödel-style limits on formal self-reference be legitimately generalized from axiomatic systems to real agent-like systems? The conjecture assumes that limits on internal derivation map onto limits of self-knowledge in cognitive or agential architectures, but it remains open which structural features are essential for the analogy and which make it misleading.

What is the precise boundary between 'mechanical self-description' and 'complete reflective closure'? The conjecture treats this distinction as central, but it is unresolved how to formalize intermediate grades of reflective understanding, partial self-modeling, error-correction, and meta-level extension without collapsing back into standard incompleteness results or vague metaphor.

Does stepping to a stronger meta-system genuinely increase self-knowledge in a principled way, or merely relocate incompleteness to a higher level? The open problem is to characterize whether there are cumulative architectures of reflection that yield meaningful progress in self-understanding despite permanent non-closure, and what counts as progress if final closure is impossible.
